items.situation: A student approaches a librarian at the help desk.
items.speaker: I'm not sure if the printer's working, but I really need this by class.

options.A: The class starts at ten o'clock.
options.B: Let me check — I can print it for you at the front desk if it's down.
options.C: I don't think printers are very expensive.
options.D: You should have printed it yesterday.

items.explanation: The speaker is indirectly asking for help with printing. Option B addresses the underlying request by offering to check and provide an alternative solution.

items.situation: Two students are discussing a group project deadline.
items.speaker: I thought we had until Friday, but the syllabus says Wednesday.

options.A: Yeah, I like Wednesdays better too.
options.B: The syllabus is on the course website.
options.C: Oh no — we'd better finish the analysis section tonight then.
options.D: Friday is a good day for deadlines.

items.explanation: The speaker is expressing concern about a sooner-than-expected deadline. Option C acknowledges the urgency and proposes immediate action.

items.situation: A student speaks to a professor after class.
items.speaker: Professor, I was wondering if there's any way to get an extension on the essay.

options.A: The essay topic is about climate change.
options.B: I finished my essay last week.
options.C: What's the situation? If you have a valid reason, we can work something out.
options.D: Extensions are usually two pages long.

items.explanation: The student is politely requesting an extension. Option C is the most appropriate response as it acknowledges the request and opens a discussion about whether it can be granted.

items.situation: asking about printing at the computer lab
items.speaker: I'm not sure if the printer's working, but I really need this by class.

options.A: The class starts in ten minutes.
options.B: Let me check the status for you, or you could try the library printer.
options.C: I need to print something too.
options.D: Yes, the computer lab is open.

items.explanation: B correctly addresses the speaker's concern by offering help and a practical alternative. A responds only to the literal mention of 'class' but ignores the concern. C ignores the speaker's specific problem by shifting focus. D is a word trap, responding literally to 'computer lab' but not to the concern about the printer.

items.situation: asking about the grading rubric for an essay
items.speaker: So the professor wants more analysis and less summary, right?

options.A: The essay is due on Friday.
options.B: Exactly, that's what I understood from the feedback.
options.C: I prefer writing summaries.
options.D: The professor is in her office.

items.explanation: B directly agrees with and confirms the speaker's understanding. A ignores the point about the rubric's content. C is an off-topic personal opinion. D is a word trap, responding to 'professor' but not to the question about the rubric.

items.situation: arranging a ride with a friend
items.speaker: If you're heading to the mall anyway, I could really use a lift.

options.A: The mall has great stores.
options.B: Sure, I'm leaving in about five minutes.
options.C: I don't have a driver's license.
options.D: Lifting weights is good exercise.

items.explanation: B agrees to the implied request for a ride. A responds to the literal topic of the mall but ignores the request. C is an off-topic statement that doesn't engage with the speaker's point. D is a word trap, misinterpreting 'lift' as physical exercise.

items.situation: discussing a cafeteria meal with a friend
items.speaker: Wow, they actually put real mushrooms in the pasta today.

options.A: I don't like mushrooms.
options.B: I know, it's a nice change from the usual canned ones.
options.C: The pasta is served at noon.
options.D: Real food is better than fake food.

items.explanation: B acknowledges and shares the speaker's surprised observation. A shifts the topic to a personal preference. C responds only to the literal word 'pasta' with irrelevant information. D is a word trap on 'real' but states an obvious, generic fact that doesn't engage with the specific surprise.

items.situation: clarifying instructions for a lab report
items.speaker: We just need to turn in the data charts, not the full analysis, correct?

options.A: The lab report is very important.
options.B: No, the full analysis is required as well.
options.C: Charts are usually in color.
options.D: Yes, that's right—just the charts by Friday.

items.explanation: D directly confirms the speaker's understanding. A is an off-topic, generic statement. B contradicts the speaker instead of confirming. C responds literally to 'charts' but ignores the request for confirmation about the instructions.

items.situation: ordering coffee at a campus café
items.speaker: It's kind of chilly in here—I think I'll get a large hot chocolate instead.

options.A: The temperature outside is fifty degrees.
options.B: Good idea, a warm drink sounds perfect.
options.C: I don't like chocolate.
options.D: Large is the biggest size they have.

items.explanation: B agrees with the speaker's implied suggestion that a warm drink is suitable. A responds literally to 'chilly' with an irrelevant fact. C shifts focus to a personal preference. D is a word trap on 'large' but gives obvious, unhelpful information.

items.situation: asking about a campus event at the student center
items.speaker: The flyer says the guest lecture is free for students, which is pretty great.

options.A: The student center closes at ten.
options.B: It really is—I was worried there'd be a fee.
options.C: I never read flyers.
options.D: Lectures are usually educational.

items.explanation: B agrees with and expands on the speaker's positive comment. A responds literally to 'student center' but ignores the comment. C is an off-topic personal remark. D is a word trap on 'lecture' but states a generic, irrelevant fact.

items.situation: asking about an assignment deadline extension
items.speaker: I heard some people got extra time on the research paper—is that true?

options.A: Research papers are difficult.
options.B: Yes, but you have to ask the professor directly before Friday.
options.C: I don't have time to talk.
options.D: The paper is about climate change.

items.explanation: B correctly interprets the query as an indirect request for information on how to get an extension. A is an off-topic, generic statement. C dismisses the conversation entirely. D is a word trap on 'research paper' but provides irrelevant content details.

items.situation: ordering coffee at a campus café
items.speaker: Speaking of exams, I should probably get a coffee with an extra shot.

options.A: Exams are next week.
options.B: Yeah, me too—a large black coffee, please.
options.C: I don't take shots.
options.D: Coffee is made from beans.

items.explanation: B appropriately follows the topic shift to ordering coffee. A sticks to the previous topic of exams (literal meaning). C is a word trap, misinterpreting 'shot' as an alcoholic drink. D states an obvious, irrelevant fact about coffee.

items.situation: talking to a classmate about a group project
items.speaker: So we're all meeting at the library tomorrow at two to finish the slides?

options.A: The library has many books.
options.B: Two o'clock is a good time for me.
options.C: That's the plan—I'll send a reminder.
options.D: Slides are usually made on a computer.

items.explanation: C directly confirms the meeting details. A is a word trap on 'library' but gives irrelevant information. B responds only to the time but doesn't confirm the overall plan. D responds literally to 'slides' with an obvious fact.

items.situation: asking about intramural sports sign-up
items.speaker: I was thinking about joining the soccer league, but I'm not sure I can commit to every game.

options.A: Soccer is a great sport to play.
options.B: The sign-up deadline is actually tomorrow.
options.C: Yeah, the schedule is pretty demanding. Maybe try the casual drop-in sessions instead?
options.D: I'm not committed to any league either.

items.explanation: C is correct because it addresses the speaker's implied excuse/hesitation by offering a helpful, less-committal alternative. A responds only to the literal mention of soccer. B ignores the speaker's point about commitment and states a fact. D uses the word 'committed' from the speaker's sentence but is an irrelevant statement about the responder.

items.situation: asking about the grading rubric for an essay
items.speaker: I'm a bit confused about what the professor is looking for in our final paper.

options.A: The final paper is due next Friday.
options.B: Confusion is a normal part of the writing process.
options.C: I saved the rubric she posted online. Want me to go over it with you?
options.D: I'm looking for a new professor too.

items.explanation: C is correct because it directly offers help in response to the speaker's expressed confusion. A is an off-topic statement of fact. B responds to the literal word 'confused' but doesn't address the need for clarification. D is a word trap on 'looking for'.

items.situation: discussing weekend plans with a roommate
items.speaker: You didn't have to clean the whole kitchen, but wow, it looks amazing.

options.A: The kitchen was very dirty.
options.B: I'm planning to clean it again next week.
options.C: Thanks! I had some free time and thought I'd surprise you.
options.D: I don't usually like cleaning.

items.explanation: C is correct because it accepts the thanks/praise implied in the speaker's statement. A and B are off-topic, factual responses that ignore the expression of gratitude. D is a literal but irrelevant response to the action mentioned.

items.situation: talking to a classmate about a group project
items.speaker: Any chance you could take the lead on the presentation slides?

options.A: I don't know how to make slides.
options.B: Presentations are due next Monday.
options.C: I'm swamped with my other class right now. Could someone else do it?
options.D: The lead singer of that band is great.

items.explanation: C is correct as it politely declines the request by giving a reason and suggesting an alternative. A is a literal, direct refusal without politeness. B is an off-topic factual statement. D is a word trap on 'lead'.

items.situation: asking a question about the lecture material
items.speaker: So, we're basically saying that the economic model failed because of external factors?

options.A: The lecture was about economic models.
options.B: Failed models are a common topic.
options.C: Exactly, that's the key point the professor was making.
options.D: I think external factors are very important.

items.explanation: C is correct because it confirms and agrees with the speaker's understanding. A and B are off-topic, generic statements about the lecture. D responds to the literal phrase 'external factors' but doesn't directly confirm the speaker's summary.

items.situation: talking to a landlord about a lease renewal
items.speaker: I got the renewal notice. The rent increase is a bit more than I was expecting.

options.A: I expect you to pay rent on time.
options.B: Notices are usually sent by mail.
options.C: I understand. The market rates have gone up, but we can discuss the timing.
options.D: I never get notices I expect.

items.explanation: C is correct because it acknowledges the tenant's concern and opens a dialogue. A is an off-topic, authoritative statement. B responds literally to 'notice' but ignores the concern. D is a word trap on 'expecting'.

items.situation: asking the registrar about course enrollment
items.speaker: I know the add-drop period ended, but my advisor just approved this change yesterday.

options.A: The add-drop period is always very short.
options.B: Advisors need to approve all changes.
options.C: I see. Let me check if we can make an exception given the timing.
options.D: I don't approve of changes either.

items.explanation: C is correct because it addresses the speaker's implied excuse/reason for a late request by offering to help. A and B are off-topic factual statements. D is a word trap on 'approved'.

items.situation: clarifying instructions for a lab report
items.speaker: Just to be clear, we're supposed to submit the data charts separately, right?

options.A: The lab report instructions are very clear.
options.B: Data charts can be difficult to make.
options.C: Right, as an appendix at the end of the main document.
options.D: I submitted my report separately.

items.explanation: C is correct because it confirms and agrees with the speaker's understanding, adding helpful detail. A and B are off-topic comments. D is a literal response to 'submit' and 'separately' but doesn't confirm the speaker's question.

items.situation: talking to a store clerk about a return
items.speaker: I'm really glad you could exchange this without the original receipt.

options.A: The original receipt is usually required.
options.B: Our return policy is posted on the wall.
options.C: You're welcome. I could see it was a recent purchase.
options.D: I don't have the receipt either.

items.explanation: C is correct because it accepts the thanks implied in the speaker's statement. A and B are off-topic, policy-focused responses that ignore the gratitude. D is a literal, self-focused response to part of the statement.

items.situation: talking to a lab partner about an experiment
items.speaker: We should probably run the calibration test one more time before we record the final data.

options.A: The calibration test is the first step.
options.B: Final data is recorded in the blue notebook.
options.C: Good idea. Better to be sure everything's accurate.
options.D: I don't have time to run right now.

items.explanation: C is correct because it confirms and agrees with the speaker's suggestion. A and B are off-topic, literal facts about the procedure. D is a word trap on 'run'.

items.situation: checking in at the campus health center
items.speaker: I filled out the form, but I'm not sure what to do next.

options.A: The form is on the clipboard over there.
options.B: I'm not feeling very well either.
options.C: You should probably just take a seat and wait for your name to be called.
options.D: I'm not sure what time they close.

items.explanation: The speaker is implicitly asking for guidance on the next step. C directly addresses this by giving appropriate advice. A responds literally to the mention of the form. B ignores the speaker's point about procedure and focuses on feeling unwell. D is off-topic, introducing an unrelated question about hours.

items.situation: asking about an assignment deadline extension
items.speaker: I was wondering if there's any chance I could turn in the paper a day late.

options.A: I turned mine in yesterday, actually.
options.B: I'm afraid the deadline is firm for everyone.
options.C: A day has twenty-four hours.
options.D: Yes, the paper is about chance and probability.

items.explanation: The speaker is making a polite request for an extension. B appropriately and politely refuses this request. A ignores the speaker's point by talking about their own submission. C responds literally to the words 'a day'. D is a word trap on 'chance'.

items.situation: discussing weekend plans with a roommate
items.speaker: So, are you still thinking of going to that concert on Saturday?

options.A: I think concerts are usually on weekends.
options.B: Actually, I was going to ask if you wanted to see a movie instead.
options.C: Yes, I bought the tickets a month ago.
options.D: Saturday is the day after Friday.

items.explanation: The speaker is asking about a specific plan. B appropriately changes the topic by suggesting a different activity. C directly answers the literal question but does not change the topic. A gives a general, off-topic comment about concerts. D is a literal, irrelevant statement about days.

items.situation: asking a residence advisor about a maintenance issue
items.speaker: My desk lamp flickers every time I turn it on.

options.A: I prefer lamps that don't flicker.
options.B: You should put in a maintenance request online.
options.C: Turn it on and off a few times.
options.D: My desk is made of wood.

items.explanation: The speaker is implicitly reporting a problem and seeking a solution. B gives appropriate, actionable advice on how to get it fixed. A is an off-topic personal opinion. C gives simplistic, unhelpful advice that ignores the proper procedure. D is a word trap on 'desk'.

items.situation: discussing a reading with a classmate before class
items.speaker: The author's argument about climate policy seems pretty convincing, right?

options.A: I haven't finished the reading yet.
options.B: Absolutely, the evidence she uses is really strong.
options.C: Climate policy is an important topic.
options.D: The author wrote several other books.

items.explanation: The speaker is seeking agreement on an evaluation. B directly agrees and provides supporting reasoning. A ignores the speaker's point by stating they are unprepared. C gives a generic, off-topic agreement with the subject, not the speaker's point about the argument. D is an irrelevant fact about the author.

items.situation: talking to a store clerk about a return
items.speaker: I bought this here last week, but it doesn't fit.

options.A: This store was built last year.
options.B: Did you try it on before you bought it?
options.C: We can exchange it for a different size if you have the receipt.
options.D: It fits me perfectly.

items.explanation: The speaker is implicitly initiating a return or exchange. C appropriately suggests a solution. B ignores the implicit request and asks an unhelpful, possibly critical question. A is an off-topic statement about the store. D is a word trap on 'fit' and is irrelevant.

items.situation: discussing a study group meeting time
items.speaker: I've got a lab that ends at four, so I can't make the three-thirty meeting.

options.A: Labs are usually in the science building.
options.B: We could take notes for you and send them after.
options.C: I have a meeting at three-thirty too.
options.D: Four is later than three-thirty.

items.explanation: The speaker is stating a scheduling conflict. B offers a helpful solution to include the speaker. C shares a similar problem but does not offer help, ignoring the speaker's point. A gives an off-topic fact about labs. D states the literal, obvious time difference.

items.situation: asking about an assignment deadline extension
items.speaker: Would it be possible to get an extra day for the project?

options.A: It's possible the deadline is tomorrow.
options.B: Projects usually take a long time.
options.C: I'm sorry, but extensions aren't allowed this time.
options.D: An extra day would be twenty-four hours.

items.explanation: The speaker is making a direct, polite request. C politely and directly refuses. A is a word trap on 'possible' and gives an unhelpful literal response. B gives an off-topic general comment. D responds literally to 'extra day'.

items.situation: calling to check business hours
items.speaker: I'm just calling to see if you're open past six tonight.

options.A: We close at eight, so yes, thank you for calling.
options.B: I'm open to new ideas.
options.C: Tonight, I'm going to a movie.
options.D: Six is a number between five and seven.

items.explanation: The speaker is asking for information. A provides the information and appropriately thanks the caller for their inquiry. B is a word trap on 'open' with an irrelevant meaning. C is an off-topic personal statement. D gives a literal, pointless fact about the number six.

items.situation: talking to a tutor about writing help
items.speaker: I'm really struggling to make my thesis statement clearer.

options.A: Let's look at it together and see if we can sharpen it.
options.B: You should struggle less with your writing.
options.C: Clear statements are important in writing.
options.D: My thesis is about ancient history.

items.explanation: The speaker is expressing a problem and implicitly asking for help. A agrees with the need to work on it and offers collaborative help. B gives unhelpful, literal advice that ignores the request. C states an off-topic, generic truth about writing. D is a word trap on 'thesis' and is irrelevant.
